posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
The first bottle, opened a couple ago was corked, so we moved on to other wines. Today I opened a second bottle, and was initially disappointed. The color was deep is red, and a few stray wisps coming from the decanter promised much, but when I poured a glass, it seemed drab and not very interesting. It was a bit like a pissed off horse going round a jump circuit and ending up in last place.

I left the glass on the table, started watching the original version of the Lion in Winter, and basically forgot about the wine. Halfway through the movie, I took a sip. It had opened up; there was complexity, some mid palate lift, and the blahness had gone. As the movie and the night progressed, the wine had opened up further still; the finish, initially short to medium, had extended to medium to long. The tertiary flavors of earth and leather had arrived to mingle with some red fruit. This is not a great wine, but it is very good, and to extract maximum pleasure, I should have decanted it early in the afternoon.

Wine 94 at its best
Lion in Winter 99

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Awesome movie, but shouldn’t you have popped a 1968 Chinon?

I know you revere these older DDCs. Not sure I’ve had one from this era. Should try to source one.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked: Come to NY. I will happily open a bottle.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: You should! From before the plantings were heavily expanded into what was formerly forest (and with a lot more Merlot planted). They’re really the DNA of DDC, which we begin to recapture in the last few vintages.

posts.name: Cameron Clark
posts.cooked: Anyone had the Blanc recently ? Given how well the 64 Blanc was at 50 years old (when I finished mine)I would expect it to be in a good place.

posts.name: Johan Berglund / The Wine Independent
posts.cooked:
Robert.A.Jr:

shouldn’t you have popped a 1968 Chinon?

I know you revere these older DDCs. Not sure I’ve had one from this era. Should try to source one.

Being a 68 myself I would be MORE than grateful for recommendations from that year and an area I know absolutely nothing about. Please lead me by the hand.

As for DDC and 1968, I have had that a couple of times thanks to the amazing kindness of Olivier Bernard and his knowledge of my poor status as a Bordeaux vintage… Both times it has shown quite far above the expectations of mine and other rather far more experienced tasters round the table.

19681920×1281 271 KB

-DDC 1968

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Sorry Johan, was a joke, parallels to the 1968 release of that movie. I’ve had some wonderful Chinons from the 1970s - Raffault in particular - but never any from the 1960s.

You’ve been dropping great photos on us lately, nice!!! Keep it up. The Bordeaux vineyard pics are fabulous.

posts.name: Johan Berglund / The Wine Independent
posts.cooked:
Robert.A.Jr:

Sorry Johan, was a joke, parallels to the 1968 release of that movie. I’ve had some wonderful Chinons from the 1970s - Raffault in particular - but never any from the 1960s.

Ah, you see for me 1968 is far too serious a matter to be made light of! Oh well, knew it was too good to be true I suppose…

And yeah, as my 18th straight en primeur visit is about to start next week, don’t be surprised if I might be piling on as far as photography is concerned!

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked: 1968 was a good year for California Cabernet. In fact a very good year.

posts.name: Johan Berglund / The Wine Independent
posts.cooked:
Mark_Golodetz:

1968 was a good year for California Cabernet. In fact a very good year.

THIS is what I keep hearing. Now if I could only find some…

posts.name: Troy Morris
posts.cooked: ‘66 is my year, I’ll have to look for some.
